text:
101
    Moitessier c. Féral, in Annales de la Propriété Industrielle, Artistique et Littéraire 23 (1878): 93-95 :
          Que la propriété elle -même reconnait des bornes établies non-seulement par la loi positive, mais
          par les convenances sociales; que la première de ces convenances consiste dans le respect dû à
          l’inviolabilité du foyer domestique; que cette inviolabilité serait atteinte si l’image de la mère de
          famille pouvait être livrée à la publicité d’enchères banales . . . .
For some further inforamtion, see Hans Naef, New Material on Ingres’ Portraits of Mme. Moitessier, The
Burlington Magazine 3 (March, 1969), 149-151 ; [Gary Tinterow], note in Portraits by Ingres : Images of
an Epoch (New York : Metropolitan Museum, 1999), 441.
102
    Comment by Vaunois to Alexandre Dumas c. Jacquet, in Annales de la Propriété Industrielle, Artistique
et Littéraire 33 (1888): 286 : “ ‘droit sacré et inalienable que nous avons sur nous-mêmes’ et, par suite, sur
la reproduction de notre figure. ”
103
    William Eden c. Whistler, Cour de Cassation, Civ. Rej. 14 mars 1900, in Recueil Dalloz 1900, 1: 500.
Whistler, Eden v. Whistler : The Baronet and the Butterfly. On this case, see François Rigaux, La
protection de la vie privée et des autres biens de la personnalité (Brussels : Bruylant, 1990), 288, 157-159.

35
         Indeed, it was in the tradition of uneasiness about the free press that privacy

received its first formal statutory protection of the nienteenth century, in 1868, during the

waning years of Napoleon III’s imperial regime. Over the course of the 1860s, the period

of the so-called “Liberal Empire,” Napoleon III granted increasing civil rights in France.

This included the creation of a new press policy in 1868, which, with some limitations,

allowed effective freedom of the press. The French press, meanwhile, had been

experiencing a small renaissance of critical and satirical reportage, as well as a

renaissance of French caricature in the work of artists like Daumier. 104 Attitudes had not

changed since 1819, though, and the new law aimed to guarantee that freedom of the

press would not open the door to insulting intrusions into the lives of respectable people.

Thus it carefully provided that, though the press was in principle free, every publication

in a periodical of “a fact of private life” was a criminal offense, a punishable

contravention, just as other insults were contraventions. An interpretive circular of the

Ministry of Justice added that while there was of course room for criticism of artists and

other public figures, even the latter were (as they would continue to be) protected against

“defamation and insult.” Concerns about the care of one’s dignity thus continued to

shadow press liberalization, just as they had already done in 1791.

With the fall of Napoleon III, the climate certainly changed, and when the Third

Republic turned to the regulation of the press, in 1881, its new statute said nothing about
